region_of_origin: China (Sichuan / Hunan); widely adapted across South and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for hot and sour soup. FOODON_03311737 (soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Soups and broths) is the closest parent category; no specific hot and sour soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for hot and sour soup. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of vegetarian hot and sour soup, approximately 300 g, as typically served in a Singaporean or South Asian restaurant context.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and sour soup (vegetarian, restaurant-style) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for vegetarian hot and sour soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for hot and sour soup (NDB 06416, USDA SR Legacy) and cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods entries for tofu, mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and vegetable broth. The vegetarian version omits pork; protein and fat values are adjusted downward accordingly. Sodium reflects typical restaurant-style preparation with soy sauce and broth.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable soups and broth-based soups — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable-based soups and starch-thickened broths
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian hot and sour soup. GI estimated at 35 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable soups (GI range 25–48) and starch-thickened broths. The dominant carbohydrate source is cornstarch (GI ~68 in isolation) but is present in small quantities (~2–3 g per 100 g), diluted by the high water content (~91 g per 100 g), resulting in a low overall GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 4.5 × 3) / 100 = 4.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request a reduced-sodium version, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing roughly 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate density
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 4.7 per serving, vegetarian hot and sour soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as rice or noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content (0.2 g per 100 g) is favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are following a sodium-restricted diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Hot and Sour Soup (素酸辣汤, Suan La Tang) is a broth-based soup originating from Chinese cuisine, particularly associated with Sichuan and Hunan culinary traditions, and widely adapted across South and Southeast Asia including Singapore. The soup is characterised by a sharp balance of sour notes from rice vinegar or black vinegar and heat from white pepper and chilli. The vegetarian version replaces the traditional pork or chicken with tofu, wood ear mushrooms, shiitake mushrooms, and bamboo shoots, simmered in a seasoned vegetable broth thickened with cornstarch to achieve a silky, slightly viscous texture. Soy sauce contributes umami depth and colour. The soup is low in calories (approximately 35 kcal per 100 g) and low in fat (0.9 g per 100 g), making it a relatively light starter or accompaniment. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose levels. However, sodium content is high (approximately 380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per 300 g serving) due to soy sauce and broth, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The soup is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a starter in Chinese, Indo-Chinese, and pan-Asian restaurants across Singapore and South Asia.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for vegetarian hot and sour soup was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for hot and sour soup (NDB 06416) with adjustments for the vegetarian formulation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable soups. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA source is a close analogue and the adjustments are conservative and well-documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 06416, hot and sour soup) adjusted for vegetarian formulation (no pork). Protein and fat values are slightly lower than the non-vegetarian version. 2. Several micronutrients are reported as -1 because they are not reported in the source database (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 06416) for this food category and cannot be defensibly estimated. Specifically: vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 3. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg; this is confirmed zero for the vegetarian version as no animal-derived fat sources are present. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; confirmed zero as no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation. 5. GI is set to 35 (low) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database vegetable soup entries; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 6. Sodium content is high and represents the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. 7. The restaurant context (Aryana — North Indian Restaurant) suggests an Indo-Chinese or pan-Asian adaptation of the classic Chinese hot and sour soup; the vegetarian formulation is consistent with North Indian restaurant offerings. 8. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish requirements (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore.
